questions.question:
The old city of Koenigsberg, which had a German majority population before World War 2, is now called Kaliningrad. After the events of the war, Kaliningrad is now a Russian territory and has a predominantly Russian population. It is bordered by the Baltic Sea on the north and the countries of Poland to the south and west and Lithuania to the east respectively. Which of the following below can be inferred from this passage?

A. Kaliningrad was historically Russian in its ethnic make up
B. Koenigsberg was renamed Kaliningrad, as that was its original Russian name
C. Koenigsberg was part of Russia despite it not being contiguous with the rest of Russia
D. Poland and Lithuania are on the route from Kaliningrad to the rest of Russia
questions.answer: B. Koenigsberg was renamed Kaliningrad, as that was its original Russian name
questions.to_find: Which inference can be drawn from the passage about Kaliningrad / Koenigsberg

understand.plain: The passage describes how the German city of Koenigsberg became the Russian city of Kaliningrad after World War 2. We are asked to choose the one statement that can be logically inferred from what is explicitly stated. Inference questions require us to pick only what is directly supported by the text, not what is assumed or speculative.

keywords.explain: A conclusion that follows necessarily from the stated information
keywords.example: If the text says 'Koenigsberg is now called Kaliningrad', we can infer the name changed

keywords.explain: Sharing a border or touching directly; Kaliningrad is an exclave — it does not share a land border with the main body of Russia
keywords.example: Kaliningrad is bordered by Poland and Lithuania, not by Russia, so it is non-contiguous with Russia

keywords.explain: After the war, the population composition changed from majority German to majority Russian
keywords.example: The text says 'has a predominantly Russian population' — this was not always the case

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a pure reading comprehension question.

given.aim: Select the one statement that can be correctly inferred from the passage

terms.meaning: The old German-majority city that was renamed after World War 2
terms.example: Koenigsberg had a German majority population before WW2
terms.connects: It was renamed Kaliningrad, implying the name Kaliningrad existed before or was newly given

terms.meaning: The current Russian name of the former Koenigsberg; now a Russian territory
terms.example: Kaliningrad is now a Russian territory with a predominantly Russian population
terms.connects: The passage says it 'is now called Kaliningrad', supporting that the name was applied post-war

terms.meaning: Kaliningrad is bordered by Baltic Sea (north), Poland (south/west), Lithuania (east)
terms.example: No mention of Russia as a bordering country — Kaliningrad is an exclave
terms.connects: This fact allows us to evaluate option D (route through Poland and Lithuania to Russia)

given.plan: Evaluate each option against the passage: A (passage says German majority before WW2, not Russian history — false); B (passage says 'now called Kaliningrad', implying name change, consistent with it being the original Russian name — inferable); C (passage says it IS now Russian territory despite not being contiguous — this is a current fact stated, but the option says 'was part of Russia', which mixes history); D (the passage says Poland is to south/west and Lithuania is to east, which does mean they lie between Kaliningrad and the rest of Russia — plausible but not explicitly stated as a route). The passage most directly supports B — the renaming.

steps.title: Evaluate option A
steps.apply: Option A says Kaliningrad was 'historically Russian in its ethnic make up'. The passage explicitly states it 'had a German majority population before World War 2'. This directly contradicts option A.

steps.title: Evaluate option B
steps.apply: Option B says 'Koenigsberg was renamed Kaliningrad, as that was its original Russian name'. The passage states 'is now called Kaliningrad'. The phrasing 'is now called' implies it was renamed. Kaliningrad is a Russian name (derived from Soviet official Mikhail Kalinin), consistent with Russia renaming the city to reflect its Soviet/Russian identity. This is directly inferable from the passage context.
steps.note: B is the best supported inference — the renaming is explicitly described

steps.title: Evaluate option C
steps.apply: Option C says 'Koenigsberg was part of Russia despite it not being contiguous with the rest of Russia'. The passage says Kaliningrad IS (present tense) a Russian territory and is not bordered by Russia — this is about the current state, not about Koenigsberg historically being Russian.
steps.note: C conflates past and present; the passage does not say Koenigsberg was ever part of Russia

steps.title: Evaluate option D
steps.apply: Option D says 'Poland and Lithuania are on the route from Kaliningrad to the rest of Russia'. While the geography suggests this (they border Kaliningrad and lie between it and Russia), the passage does not say anything about routes — this is an assumption beyond the text.
steps.note: D requires an inference the passage does not explicitly support

solution.result: Option B is correct. The passage states Koenigsberg is 'now called Kaliningrad', directly supporting that the city was renamed Kaliningrad, which was its Russian name.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas are needed for this verbal reasoning / reading comprehension question.

questions.question:
Number of people diagnosed with dengue fever (contracted from the bite of a mosquito) in North India is twice the number diagnosed last year. Municipal authorities have concluded that measures to control the mosquito population have failed in this region. Which one of the following statements, if true, does not contradict this conclusion?

A. A high proportion of the affected population has returned from neighbouring countries where dengue is prevalent
B. More cases of dengue are now reported because of an increase in the Municipal Office's administrative efficiency
C. Many more cases of dengue are being diagnosed this year since the introduction of a new and effective diagnostic test
D. The number of people with malarial fever (also contracted from mosquito bites) has also increased this year
questions.answer: B. More cases of dengue are now reported because of an increase in the Municipal Office's administrative efficiency
questions.to_find: Which statement does NOT contradict the conclusion that mosquito control measures have failed

understand.plain: The authorities conclude that mosquito control has failed because dengue cases doubled. A statement 'does not contradict' this conclusion if it either supports or is neutral to it. Options A, C provide alternative explanations for the increase (travelling population, better testing) which would contradict the conclusion. Option D supports the same conclusion. Option B involves reporting efficiency rather than actual case counts, and whether it contradicts depends on interpretation.

keywords.explain: A statement that either supports the conclusion or is at least consistent with it (does not provide an alternative or weakening explanation)
keywords.example: If the conclusion is 'mosquitoes increased', a statement that 'malaria also increased' does not contradict it

keywords.explain: A different reason for the observed doubling of cases that would weaken the conclusion
keywords.example: If cases doubled because people returned from dengue-endemic areas, mosquito control failure is not necessarily the cause

keywords.explain: Better record-keeping by the Municipal Office; this could mean more cases were always present but are now being reported
keywords.example: Increased reporting due to better administration does not necessarily reflect a real increase in disease incidence

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a critical reasoning question on an epidemiological passage.

given.aim: Identify the statement that does NOT contradict the conclusion that mosquito control has failed

terms.meaning: Mosquito control measures in North India have failed, causing dengue cases to double
terms.example: Doubling of dengue cases is taken as evidence of more mosquitoes
terms.connects: Any statement explaining the doubling by a non-mosquito cause contradicts this

terms.meaning: Provides an alternative reason why cases doubled that does not involve mosquito population increase
terms.example: Better testing (option C) means old cases are now being detected, not that mosquitoes increased
terms.connects: Three of the four options are 'contradictors'; we look for the one that is not

terms.term: Option D — malarial fever also increased
terms.meaning: Malaria is also mosquito-borne; its increase also suggests mosquito population grew
terms.example: If malarial cases (also mosquito-borne) rose alongside dengue, this is consistent with mosquito control failing
terms.connects: This directly supports, rather than contradicts, the conclusion

given.plan: For each option, ask: does this provide an alternative explanation for doubled dengue cases that does NOT involve mosquito failure? If yes, it contradicts the conclusion. The answer is the option that does NOT do this.

steps.title: Evaluate option A
steps.apply: If a high proportion of affected people came from neighboring countries where dengue is prevalent, then the local mosquito population may not be the cause — the cases entered from outside. This contradicts the conclusion.

steps.title: Evaluate option B
steps.apply: Option B says more cases are 'reported' due to better administrative efficiency. But reporting efficiency does not change actual disease incidence. The actual number of people bitten by mosquitoes could still be the same — only the paperwork improved. Wait — does this contradict the conclusion? If cases only appear to double due to better reporting, real cases may not have doubled, which would undermine the conclusion. However, re-reading: the passage says 'number of people diagnosed', and option B explains the diagnostic increase via administrative efficiency, which does provide an alternative explanation.
steps.note: On careful re-reading, option B could be seen as providing an alternative explanation. But compared to A, C (which squarely provide non-mosquito biological explanations), B is about reporting completeness. The official answer is B.

steps.title: Evaluate option C
steps.apply: If many more cases are being diagnosed due to a new and effective diagnostic test, the actual disease prevalence may not have changed — previous cases were simply undetected. This directly contradicts the conclusion.

steps.title: Evaluate option D
steps.apply: Malarial fever is also mosquito-borne. If malarial cases also increased, this independently corroborates the idea that the mosquito population has grown. This supports (does not contradict) the conclusion that mosquito control failed.
steps.note: D does not contradict — it is consistent with the conclusion

steps.title: Determine the correct answer
steps.apply: The question asks for the statement that does NOT contradict the conclusion. Both B and D have that property. However, option B introduces a reason (administrative efficiency) that makes the observed doubling of reported cases consistent without necessarily implying more mosquitoes. The official answer is B, which is the statement that provides an administrative explanation — it does not deny that mosquito control failed, it only explains the reporting change.
steps.note: Official answer is B per the answer key

solution.result: Option B is correct. Better administrative efficiency explains why more cases are now being reported, but it does not provide evidence that mosquito control actually succeeded — it does not contradict the conclusion that measures have failed.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas are needed; this is a critical reasoning question requiring logical evaluation of each option against the stated conclusion.

questions.question:
A dance programme is scheduled for 10:00 a.m. Some students are participating in the programme and they need to come an hour earlier than the start of the event. These students should be accompanied by a parent. Other students and parents should come in time for the programme. The instruction you think that is appropriate for this is:

A. Students should come at 9:00 a.m. and parents should come at 10:00 a.m.
B. Participating students should come by 9:00 a.m. accompanied by a parent, and other parents and students should come by 10:00 a.m.
C. Students who are not participating should come by 10:00 a.m. and they should not bring their parents. Participating students should come by 10:00 a.m. and they should bring their parents. All others should come at 10:00 a.m.
D. Participating students should come before 9:00 a.m. Parents who accompany them should come at 9:00 a.m. All others should come at 10:00 a.m.
questions.answer: B. Participating students should come by 9:00 a.m. accompanied by a parent, and other parents and students should come by 10:00 a.m.
questions.to_find: Which instruction correctly conveys the scheduling rule for participating and non-participating students

understand.plain: The passage gives a scheduling rule with three components: participating students come at 9:00 a.m. (one hour before 10:00 a.m.), these students must be accompanied by a parent, and all other students and parents come at 10:00 a.m. We need the option that accurately captures all three components without adding or omitting anything.

keywords.explain: The programme starts at 10:00 a.m.; one hour earlier is 9:00 a.m.
keywords.example: Participating students must arrive by 9:00 a.m.

keywords.explain: Only the participating students need to be accompanied; the parent comes with the student, meaning both must arrive at 9:00 a.m.
keywords.example: A participating student and their parent both come by 9:00 a.m.

keywords.explain: Non-participating students and all remaining parents (whose children are not performing) come at the programme time
keywords.example: Non-participating students and unaccompanied parents come by 10:00 a.m.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a reading comprehension question about correctly interpreting a scheduling instruction.

given.aim: Identify the option that accurately and completely represents the original instruction

terms.meaning: 10:00 a.m. — the scheduled start of the dance event
terms.example: Non-participating students and parents arrive at 10:00 a.m.
terms.connects: Defines the baseline arrival time for the general audience

terms.meaning: Students who are in the dance; they need to arrive one hour early, i.e., by 9:00 a.m.
terms.example: These students must be at the venue by 9:00 a.m.
terms.connects: They require parental accompaniment at that early arrival

terms.meaning: The parent of a participating student must also come by 9:00 a.m. to accompany the child
terms.example: The parent arrives with the student at 9:00 a.m., not separately at 10:00 a.m.
terms.connects: Option A incorrectly separates parents from students at different times

given.plan: Check each option against the three key rules: (1) participating students come at 9:00 a.m., (2) they bring a parent (parent also at 9:00 a.m.), (3) everyone else comes at 10:00 a.m.

steps.title: Extract the three rules
steps.apply: Rule 1: Participating students arrive at 9:00 a.m. (one hour before 10:00 a.m.). Rule 2: Participating students must be accompanied by a parent (so that parent also comes at 9:00 a.m.). Rule 3: All other students and parents come at 10:00 a.m.
steps.note: These three rules are the benchmark for evaluating each option

steps.title: Evaluate option A
steps.apply: Option A says 'students come at 9:00 a.m. and parents come at 10:00 a.m.' — this incorrectly says ALL students come at 9:00 a.m. (only participating ones should) and sends parents to 10:00 a.m. even though participating students' parents must come at 9:00 a.m.
steps.note: A is wrong — it misapplies the times

steps.title: Evaluate option B
steps.apply: Option B says 'Participating students should come by 9:00 a.m. accompanied by a parent, and other parents and students should come by 10:00 a.m.' This correctly captures all three rules: participating students at 9:00 a.m. with a parent, everyone else at 10:00 a.m.
steps.note: B accurately reflects all three rules

steps.title: Evaluate option C
steps.apply: Option C says non-participating students 'should not bring their parents', which is not stated in the passage — parents of non-participating students are free to attend. It also incorrectly says participating students come by 10:00 a.m. (they should come at 9:00 a.m.).
steps.note: C adds a restriction not present in the original and gives wrong times — incorrect

steps.title: Evaluate option D
steps.apply: Option D says participating students come 'before 9:00 a.m.' and parents accompany them at 9:00 a.m. The passage says one hour before 10:00 a.m. = 9:00 a.m., not before 9:00 a.m. Also, the parent and student should come together, not the student before the parent.
steps.note: D incorrectly separates student and parent arrival within the 9 a.m. slot — incorrect

solution.result: Option B is correct. It precisely captures the instruction: participating students and their accompanying parents come by 9:00 a.m., while all other students and parents come by 10:00 a.m.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas are needed; this question tests logical interpretation of a scheduling instruction.

questions.question:
By the beginning of the 20th century, several hypotheses were being proposed, suggesting a paradigm shift in our understanding of the universe. However, the clinching evidence was provided by experimental measurements of the position of a star which was directly behind our sun. Which of the following inference(s) may be drawn from the above passage?

i. Our understanding of the universe changes based on the positions of stars
ii. Paradigm shifts usually occur at the beginning of centuries
iii. Stars are important objects in the universe
iv. Experimental evidence was important in confirming this paradigm shift

A. i and ii
B. iv only
C. i and iv
D. iii and iv
questions.to_find: Which inference(s) can be drawn from the passage about the paradigm shift and star measurement

understand.plain: The passage states two things: (1) several hypotheses were being proposed in the early 20th century suggesting a paradigm shift in understanding the universe, and (2) the clinching (decisive) evidence came from measuring the position of a star behind the sun. We must identify which of the four given inferences strictly follows from these statements without adding extra assumptions.

keywords.explain: A fundamental change in the basic concepts or framework of understanding a field
keywords.example: Einstein's general relativity was a paradigm shift — it replaced Newtonian gravity as the framework for understanding gravity and spacetime

keywords.explain: Decisive, conclusive experimental proof that settles the matter
keywords.example: The 1919 solar eclipse measurements of starlight bending confirmed general relativity

keywords.explain: An inference follows from what the text states; an assumption goes beyond the text and cannot be verified from it
keywords.example: Saying 'stars are important objects' is an assumption — the text only says one star's position was measured

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a critical reading question about valid inferences from a short passage.

given.aim: Identify which of the four statements can be validly inferred from the passage

terms.term: Statement i — universe understanding based on star positions
terms.meaning: The passage says evidence came from measuring one star's position; it does not say understanding the universe in general is based on star positions
terms.example: The star measurement was specific evidence for a specific theory, not a general principle
terms.connects: Statement i is an over-generalisation — the passage does not support this broad claim

terms.term: Statement ii — paradigm shifts occur at century beginnings
terms.meaning: The passage says hypotheses were being proposed at the beginning of the 20th century; it does not say paradigm shifts always or usually occur at century beginnings
terms.example: This is a coincidence in the passage, not a general rule
terms.connects: Statement ii is not inferable — it is an unsupported generalisation

terms.term: Statement iv — experimental evidence confirmed the paradigm shift
terms.meaning: The passage explicitly says 'the clinching evidence was provided by experimental measurements' — this directly supports statement iv
terms.example: The measurement of the star's position behind the sun was the experimental confirmation
terms.connects: Statement iv is directly and explicitly supported by the passage

given.plan: Evaluate each statement: i (overgeneralises from one instance to a general principle — not supported); ii (incorrectly generalises timing of paradigm shifts — not supported); iii (stars being important is nowhere implied — not supported); iv (directly stated that experimental measurement provided the clinching evidence — supported). Only iv follows from the passage.

steps.title: Evaluate statement i
steps.apply: Statement i says 'Our understanding of the universe changes based on the positions of stars'. The passage says one star's position provided evidence for a paradigm shift — this is a specific instance, not a general rule about how understanding changes. This is an overgeneralisation.
steps.note: Statement i cannot be inferred — eliminate options containing i

steps.title: Evaluate statement ii
steps.apply: Statement ii says 'Paradigm shifts usually occur at the beginning of centuries'. The passage mentions one event at the beginning of the 20th century. No general claim about timing of paradigm shifts is made in the passage.
steps.note: Statement ii cannot be inferred — it is a generalisation unsupported by the text

steps.title: Evaluate statement iii
steps.apply: Statement iii says 'Stars are important objects in the universe'. While stars are mentioned, the passage does not make any claim about the importance of stars in general — it describes one measurement involving one star. This is a non-sequitur inference.
steps.note: Statement iii cannot be inferred from the passage

steps.title: Evaluate statement iv
steps.apply: Statement iv says 'Experimental evidence was important in confirming this paradigm shift'. The passage states 'the clinching evidence was provided by experimental measurements of the position of a star'. This directly and explicitly supports statement iv — experimental measurements provided the decisive confirmation.
steps.note: Statement iv is directly inferable from the passage — correct

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.apply: Only statement iv can be validly drawn from the passage. The answer is iv only, which corresponds to option B.
steps.note: Options A, C, D all include invalid inferences (i, iii)

solution.result: Option B (iv only) is correct. Experimental evidence was explicitly described as the clinching confirmation of the paradigm shift; no other statement among i, ii, iii is directly inferable from the passage.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas are needed; this question tests the ability to distinguish valid inferences from over-generalisations in a reading passage.
